text:
Admission Date:  2008-01-22              Discharge Date:   1999-01-25

Date of Birth:  2001-07-11             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Zachary
Chief Complaint:
Alterred mental status

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Endotracheal Intubation and Extubation


History of Present Illness:
71yoM with h/o GOLD stage III COPD and moderate pulm HTN on last
echo, schizophrenia, cryptogenic vs EtOH cirrhosis, and prostate
ca on Lupron presents with several day history of SOB. Unable to
give history. John Stuart Stevenson (program nurse where he lives 56-615-3748), over the past week, looking more short of breath. He
refused to go see his doctor. Yesterday, he was out on the back
deck in the pouring rain smoking (heavy smoker, 2 ppd) and this
morning he fell in his room, onto his knees, no LOC or head hit.
Unable to get up, was much slower and more confused, didn't know
what date it was, thought it was 1980. No fevers, chills,
sweats, n/v/d/abd pain, CP, palpitations. Face looked very
puffy. Nothing really much more on ROS or history.
.
Initial ED vitals: 99.3  p100  110/94  20  100%NRB. He was noted
to have bilateral LE edema, crackly on exam, but mentating well
and following commands. Initally 88% on NRB, Bipap'ed him with
improvement in his symptoms and saturation. The first ABG before
Bipap showed acute respiratory acidosis with pH 7.29/56/76/28 on
unclear O2. Bipap was felt to have improved his symptoms. CXR
showed mild pulmonary edema and low lung volumes.
.
Labs were signficiant for severe hypoNa to 108, K 8.3 but
hemolyzed (repeat 5.1), normal renal function, leukocytosis to
12.3 with 2% bands, new thrombocytopenia to 81, lactate 1.3 -->
0.7.
.
Pt was given ASA 325, Methylpred 125 mg IV x1, Levaquin 750 mg
IV, Albuterol/Ipratropium nebs x1, Vancomycin 1g IV x1,
Ceftriaxone 1g IV x1, Flagyl 500 mg IV x1, IV Fentanyl, 100 mg
IV Lasix.
.
Vitals before transfer: 99.3  p105  127/85  19  100% on PS 5/5
TV's 600-700's.
.
Of note, pt was last admitted in 08/2018 for hypoxia to 83%RA at
a group home, had a CTA showing LUL tree-in-[**Male First Name (un) 239**] (inflammatory vs
infectious), dilated PA. He did not improve initially, and
repeat CXR showed new RLL PNA. He completed 7d for HAP with
Vanc/Merrem. He was discharged with 2L O2 requirement to be
weaned. He was noted to be wheezy and received nebs and short
Prednisone burst with rapid improvement. He had eleavated LFT's
on admission that trended down; RUQ u/s showed splenomegaly and
trace ascites but patent portal vasculature.
.
Further review of OMR notes shows an admission 08/1979 for
hyponatremia, increased LE swelling that improved with Lasix
diuresis. He was then found to be obtunded, febrile, hypoxic; he
was intubated for hypoxic/hypercarbic respiratory failure;
sputum cultures grew strep pneumo and bronch showed thick plugs
in R airways with purulent drainage. He was treated with
Vanc/Levo/Zosyn --> Cefepime x8 d. He was hyponatremic at that
time as well, which was thought to be cirrhotic in etiology; he
was fluid restricted and diuresed.
.
ROS: Unable to be obtained at present

Past Medical History:
- Schizophrenia - follwed by Dr. Griffin at Devereux Treatment Network/Mass Mental
- Cryptogenic cirrhosis (OMR notes indicate EtOH cirrhosis) with
portal HTN, splenomegaly, periportal varices
- Pyruvate kinase deficiency c/b splenomegaly, macrocytic anemia
- Moderate pulmonary HTN
- Castrate sensitive prostate Ca (on lupron since 1992)
- COPD
- Mediastinal LN's noted on CTA 08/2018
- Large inguinoscrotal hernia
- Splenomegaly
- Compression fracture s/p vertebroplasty
- H/o LUL and RLL pneumonias

Social History:
Lives at a group home for his schizophrenia ([**Street Address(1) 65648**])
which has help daily, but not at night. 20 PY smoker, still
currently smoking. Reports prior history of etoh abuse,
approximately 10 beers per day for about 20 years. Denies
current etoh or IVDU.  Ex-wife Trevor Moore Raymond Wilson cell 726-872-5414,
pager 418-440-8342


Family History:
He has 4 sisters that he does not keep in regular contact with.
Unsure of what his parents died from.


Physical Exam:
ADMISSION
97.2  102  115/46  17  96% on Bipap 8/5, 50%
Lethargic appearing gentleman who wakes up to voice and answers
questions mostly appropriately but not always
EOMI, light scleral icterus
JVD difficult to assess
Poor air movement, but gross expiratory wheezes heard on the L
RRR, no gross murmurs, 2+ radials and DP's
Abd soft NT ND, prominent midline surgical scar
Large left inguinoscrotal hernia noted
Neuro exam unable to be done due to mental status

DISCHARGE
VS: 97.0 102/54 72 20 92%1L
GEN: NAD, comfortable
HEENT: PERRL, EOMI, MMM
Neck: Supple, no JVD, no LAD
Pulm: Decreased BS throughout w dry crackles but improved from
prior, otherwise w/o focal findings
CV: RRR, no m/r/g
ABD: soft, NT/ND, naBS
EXT: WWP, 2+ DP/PT/radial trace pedal edema
Neuro: AOx3, CNII-XII wnl, moving all extremities

Respiratory (pre-intubation)

pH-7.26* calTCO2-34* Base XS-2

Reports
VIDEO OROPHARYNGEAL SWALLOW 1990-06-29
Video oropharyngeal swallow was performed with multiple
consistencies of barium in conjunction with the speech pathology
team. Aspiration and penetration was noted for both thin liquids
and nectar.  For additional information, please see
speech-language pathology note in web OMR.


Brief Hospital Course:
HOSPITAL COURSE
This is a 71yo M with PMHx COPD who presented with respiratory
failure, s/p ICU stay for intubation/extubation, requiring
diuresis for fluid overload, steroids for COPD exacerbation,
treatment for CAP, now w slowly respiratory status
.
ACTIVE
#. Hypercarbic Hypoxic Respiratory Failure: This patient
presented with respiratory distress in the setting of
leukocytosis and appearing volume overloaded; patient required
intubation for hypoxia secondary to acute diastolic failure and
COPD exacerbation.  She was started on steroid pulse, aggressive
diuresis, and antibiotics.  Patient respiratory status improved,
resulting in extubation; patient continued to improve; at time
of discharge patient was satting low 90s on RA and had been
diuresed to reported dry weight 163lb; patient discharged on
prednisone taper.  Given brisk response to diuresis, patient was
discharged on decreased dosing of PO lasix, and continued
spironolactone dosing.  Continued advair, nebs.
.
INACTIVE
#. Schizophrenia:  Pt followed by Dr. Griffin at Devereux Treatment Network/Mass
Mental.  Continued Risperidone 2mg qhs, risperdal consta IM
37.5mg qweek (due 09-31), benztropine 1mg daily.
.
#. Prostate Cancer:  Held lupron as not available for
inpatients, instructed to discuss w outpatient oncologist prior
to restarting.
.
TRANSITIONAL
1. Code status: Patient remained full code for duration of
hospitalization
2. Pending: No labs/studies pending at time of discharge
3. Transition of Care: Patient discharged to rehab with copy of
discharge summary
4. Barriers to Care: Patient appears to have little insight into
his seriousness of his disease process, shows no interest in
stopping smoking

Medications on Admission:
- Alendronate 70mg qweek
- Benztropine 1mg daily
- Advair (250 mcg-50 mcg) 1 puff Devereux Treatment Network
- Lasix 40mg daily
- Miralax 17 gram daily
- Risperidone 3mg qHS
- Risperdone Consta 37.5 mg IM q2weeks
- Spironolactone 150mg daily
- Tiotropium 18 mcg daily
- Calcium 500mg Devereux Treatment Network
- Cholecalciferol 1,000 unit daily
- Colace 100mg Devereux Treatment Network
- Magnesium Oxide 400mg Devereux Treatment Network
- Multivitamin
- Iron 150mg daily
- NaCl 1 gram Devereux Treatment Network
- Thiamine 100mg daily

Discharge Medications:
1. alendronate 70 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a week.

2. benztropine 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.
3. Advair Diskus 250-50 mcg/dose Disk with Device Sig: One (1)
Inhalation twice a day.
4. furosemide 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

5. Miralax 17 gram/dose Powder Sig: One (1) dose PO once a day.

6. risperidone 2 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO at bedtime.
7. Risperdal Consta 37.5 mg/2 mL Syringe Sig: One (1) syringe
Intramuscular q2weeks.

(Daily).

One (1)  Inhalation once a day.
10. calcium carbonate 200 mg calcium (500 mg) Tablet, Chewable
Sig: One (1) Tablet, Chewable PO DAILY (Daily).
11. cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1,000 unit Tablet Sig: One (1)
Tablet PO once a day.

(2 times a day).
13. magnesium oxide 400 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
14. multivitamin,tx-minerals     Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO
DAILY (Daily).
15. ferrous sulfate 300  mg (60 mg iron) Tablet Sig: One (1)
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
16. thiamine HCl 100 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
17. albuterol sulfate 2.5 mg /3 mL (0.083 %) Solution for
Nebulization Sig: One (1) neb Inhalation Q2H (every 2 hours) as
needed for shortness of breath or wheezing.
18. metoprolol tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID
(2 times a day).
19. prednisone 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily)
for 2 days.
20. prednisone 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day
for 2 days: to be started after completion of 20mg daily course.



Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Virtua West Jersey Hospital - Voorhees LivingCenter - 405 Underwood Wall Apt. 598
Nicholasport, LA 55824-9271

Discharge Diagnosis:
PRIMARY
COPD Exacerbation
Acute Diastolic Heart Failure


Discharge Condition:
Respiratory Status: O2 Saturation low 90s on 1L nasal cannula
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker
or cane).


Discharge Instructions:
Mr. Foster--

It was a pleasure taking care of you at Florida Hospital Carrollwood.  You were admitted with shortness of breath.
This was likely due to your COPD and fluid overload.  Fluid was
taken off with medications.  Your COPD was treated with
medications.  Your improved.

**YOUR SYMPTOMS ARE LIKELY RELATED TO YOUR SMOKING. SMOKING
CIGARETTES CAN CAUSE COPD, CANCER, AND HEART DISEASE.  YOU NEED
TO STOP SMOKING.**

Given your heart failure, it is important to weigh yourself
every day, and to call your doctor if you gain more than 3 lbs
in a day or 6 lbs in a week.

During the course of your hospitalization, the following changes
were made to your medications
- DECREASED lasix
- STARTED prednisone
- STARTED metoprolol
- STARTED albuterol

Followup Instructions:
Please follow-up with your doctors Melissa Bell, and
schedule an appointment with your primary care physician when
you are discharged from Bell.
